section_name: Land Reform as a Panacea? Evidence from Rural Protests in Latin America 1
text: Prasad Sankar Bhattacharya, Department of Economics, Deakin University; Prasad.bhattacharya@deakin.edu.au

This paper investigates a particular development policy involving land reforms and their possible mitigation effect on large-scale rural protests in Latin America. Our focus is on the long-run impact of various land reform implementations in the 20 th century (1900-2010, from Bhattacharya et al., 2019) and their plausible beneficial effect in terms of reducing massive rural protest incidents over a long-run period (1930-2008, from Albertus, 2015). In so doing, we focus on a novel mechanism involving democratic participation through Church or clergyinitiated activities like years of active Base Christian Communities and decipher their possible soothing impact on rural protests. We assemble a unique country-level dataset documenting the active years of clergy or Church induced democratic participation in Latin America 2 and analyse how such participatory activities could be linked with more land reform implementations which in turn diminishes the likelihood of large-scale protests. Protests or demonstrations are quite prevalent in the world as evidenced in the recent largescale protests in the USA for racial justice and in Sri Lanka due to economic mismanagement. The Carnegie Endowment for International Peace (carnegieendowment.org) documents that as of July 2022, more than 230 significant antigovernment protests have erupted in 110 different countries in the world. Machado, Scartascini and Tommasi (2011) link protests in seventeen Latin American countries with weak institutions. Acemoglu and Robinson (2012) and Aidt and Frank (2015) provide evidence on how political protests drive economic, social and political change over centuries. In the same vein, Albertus (2015) documents major rural protests in a number of countries in Latin America for the period 1930-2008. These demonstrations could be broadly categorized into the forms of land invasions (Dominican Republic, El Salvador, Honduras, Nicaragua, Panama, and Venezuela), strikes (Argentina, Bolivia), protests (Chile, Paraguay), demonstrations (Bolivia, Mexico), blockades (Ecuador), movement of landless rural workers and farmers (Brazil) as well as rural insurgency (Colombia, Cuba, Guatemala, Mexico, Peru, Uruguay). In terms of magnitudes, these demonstrations would in general, be termed as low magnitude incidents, as except for rural insurgency, the likelihood of human casualties is quite low. However, it is important to note that these protests could be catalysts to subsequent high intensity conflicts like guerrilla insurgencies as documented by the outcomes of such rallies in Albertus (2015, Table 2.3). Thus, even if these are low-intensity incidents, their possible long-term ramifications would be very significant. Hence, it is of first order importance to analyse if such major protests could be pacified with pertinent policy intervention. We take one such development policy tool involving land reforms, and in particular, the cumulative impact of such reform implementations and investigate how these reform implementations could act as pacifiers in diminishing protests. Land reform itself is a very important issue from the perspective of Latin America given the prevalence of large amount of land concentration due to the historical agrarian structure evolved through the colonization period (Sokoloff and Engerman, 2000). Albertus and Kaplan (2013) investigate whether land reform could be used as a strategy for reducing civil conflict in Colombia. Similarly, Albertus (2020) shows that greater land reform in Peru reduced conflict by augmenting retaliation

capacity akin to improving state capacity in the civil conflict literature (Blattman and Miguel, 2010). Given that the land reform episodes are major milestones in countries long-run trajectory (Bhattacharya et al. 2019; Albertus, 2015), it would be important to analyse the long-term consequence of such reforms on major protests witnessed in Latin America. Note that land reforms are not uniformly implemented in a number of countries in Latin America, for instance, in Argentina (Bhattacharya et al. 2019). In addition, even if there are multiple land reforms enacted over the years, they are always partially implemented as in Costa Rica, Guatemala and Honduras. Then in a number of other countries like Belize, Bolivia and Colombia, some particular land reforms are fully implemented, and others are either partially implemented or not implemented at all. 3 It is interesting to note that the land reforms are associated with a number of different objectives. We exploit these various motives of land reform implementations relevant for Latin American countries from Bhattacharya et al. (2019). These are: (i) land reform implementation as a package subsuming all different strands of reforms as illustrated in (ii) onward, (ii) reforms mandating upper limits in landholding or land ceiling, (iii) land expropriation motive where excess land over the mandated ceiling have been taken over from landlords for later reallocation to land-poor or landless people, (iv) redistribution motive where lands were redistributed to landless along with full end-user rights on land, (v) a combination of land ceiling, land expropriation and land redistribution motives of land reforms as often expropriation and redistribution are implemented together in a number of countries in Latin America, (vi) pro-poor land reforms where reforms are specifically targeted towards the poor or landless or land-poor or squatters as mentioned in the reform initiatives, so this can be treated as a package explicitly designed for the poor, (vii) tenure security improvement as the sole reform motive where the land tenure arrangements are improved without transferring physical lands and end-user rights to land, and (viii) a combination of land reform motives where end-user rights in land are given, viz., (a) consolidation type reforms where land is mainly re-parcelled into unitary blocks for improved use with end-user rights in land granted; (b) distribution of land from state-owned farms to citizens; and (c) privatization or sale of state-owned farmlands to individuals. In addition, the following land reform motives are taken from Albertus (2015). First, we use the global major/minor land reform with the major “ is defined as the expropriation and redistribution of at least 10 percent of cultivable land over a continuous period with at least one year in which more than 1 percent of cultivable land is expropriated. ” 4 Minor reforms are depicted as “ those where there was expropriation and redistribution as part of a land reform program but at a threshold short of major reform. ” The second motive is the global national land reform from Albertus (2015). Note that these motives are distinct from the motives taken from Bhattacharya et al (2019). The Church induced democratic participation, the new mechanism proposed in the paper stems from the fact that Church or clergy plays a very important role in Latin America in shaping up the social impact on development in general through civil society engagement 3 Please refer to the Supplementary Appendix of Bhattacharya et al. (2019), and especially Table A1 in regard to major land reforms enactment bills and their implementation status. 4 See notes of Albertus (2015), Table 8.1, page 273.

(Smith 2017; Linz and Stepan, 1996). Given the prominence of religion in Latin America, we investigate a channel of clergy induced democratic participation (CIDP) including years of or active years of base Christian communities (known as Comunidades Eclesiales de Base or CEB) which may have a bearing on land reform decisions and subsequent amelioration of low-intensity conflict manifested by large scale rural protests. These clergy-initiated activities span the ‘broader special public role’ dimensions which could be listed as ‘human rights monitoring, organizing labor protests, establishing cooperatives and credit unions, providing health care and education, fundraising, supporting solidarity campaigns, publishing and other reform activities’ among others (Jordan 2022; CIA Report 1986). In the empirical analysis, first we analyse whether the land reforms would act as deterrents to rural protests by looking at the implementations of reforms within a short time span using a flexible event study analysis as in Bhalotra et al. (2019). Here we employ land reforms implemented as treated and no reforms implemented as control groups within a 10-year before and after time window. Even within this short time span, we find some support that land reforms remain effective as a protest mitigation mechanism. However, this type of investigation would treat the reforms as purely exogenous which may not be the case always as protests could be initiated due to lack of reforms in some instances (Albertus, 2015; de Janvry et al, 2015). Hence, endogeneity due to reverse causality would threaten our identification strategy. Thus, to address such concerns, we resort to instrumental variable estimation. One plausible exogenous factor affecting the land reform implementations will be the impact of elite split as documented in Albertus (2015). This spilt is between landed elites and political elites where landed elites have wealth and power based on vast amounts of lands at their disposal. The political elites composed of military and civilian politicians and have the “power and organizational capacity to run the government” (Albertus, 2015, data appendix pg. 6). Based on Figure 5.2 (pg. 153, Albertus 2015) and the narrative in the data appendix of Albertus (2015), we code the years of elite split and use this as an exogenous instrument for land reform implementation decisions. Albertus (2015) demonstrates that elite split has important positive effect on land reform decisions. Given the focus of the land reforms are key milestones in a country’s long-run trajectory (Bhattacharya et al. 2019), hence these big-ticket events could be linked with the power struggles between political elites and landed elites in Latin America. Albertus (2015) documents how elite split would hasten land reform decisions based on the intuition that such reforms help consolidating the support of the stakeholders like peasants, poor people, unemployed, union members etc to remain in power. This particular characteristic of elite split makes it the relevant instrument to be used in the identification purpose in the econometric analysis. In addition, these divisions did not instigate any major rural protests (which is the dependent variable or outcome variable in the empirical analysis), and hence, excludability restriction in terms of plausible instruments should also be satisfied. Our endogeneity corrected results reveal that cumulative land reform implementations reduce protests in general. There is quite a bit of heterogeneity in regard to specific land reform motives. Amongst the reforms, the strongest mitigating impact is attributed to the redistributive and expropriating type reforms individually as well as the combination of such reforms implemented jointly. In addition, we find that tenure security improvement type

reforms which does not entail physical transfer of land and focus on enhancing tenure arrangements between land tillers and landowners also has a detrimental impact on largescale rural protests. The main results survive a number of robustness checks involving different empirical estimation techniques and falsification exercise with alternative outcome variable from the extant literature. The novel control variable used in the study, viz. , the church or clergy induced democratic participation (CIDP), provides positive impetus to land reform implementations in our first stage instrumental variable analysis. Consequently, the coefficients of CIDPs become negative in the second stage instrumental variable regressions, lending credence to the idea that active years of democratic participation also have a direct and soothing impact on largescale rural protests in addition to their indirect effects through enticing reform implementations at the first stage. In the paper’s context, the CIDP heralds in a new democratic window of opportunity. In regard to mechanisms, we pinpoint two plausible pathways involving proportion of land redistributed from Albertus (2015) and reduction in land inequality using data from Frankema (2010). First, there is strong evidence that land reforms implemented redistributed lands to the needy (could be interpreted as improving extensive margins in land holdings), thus creating more productive income earning opportunities, which in turn reduced rural protests. Second, the land inequality gini coefficients show statistically significant reductions in land holding distributions, akin to an improvement in intensive margin of land holding patterns. In line with the main results, the redistributive and expropriation type reforms as well as their combinations are associated with both higher proportions of land being redistributed as well as more reductions in land inequalities. Hence, these motives of land reform implementations are attributed to higher protest mitigations in the main analysis. Our study is related to the following strands of literature. First, there is a body of scholarship investigating whether land reforms could be used as mitigation mechanisms to reduce civil strife in a number of countries in Latin America (Albertus and Kaplan, 2013; Albertus, 2019). We contribute to this strand by analyzing rural protests akin to low-intensity conflicts within the region over a long period of time. The investigation reveals that major land reform implementations, taken cumulatively and concentrating on expropriation and redistribution of land could be used as a pacifier in dousing such low-intensity civil strife. Second, a number of papers highlight church-induced democratic participation and their impact on development in general through civil society engagement (Smith 2017; Linz and Stepan, 1996). Given the prominence of religion in Latin America, we investigate a channel of clergy induced democratic participation (CIDP) including years of or active years of CEB which may have a bearing on land reform decisions and subsequent amelioration of lowintensity conflict manifested by large scale rural protests. Our results show that CIDP hasten the reforms decisions in the first stage analysis, and especially for land redistribution mechanisms. Third, the paper is, in a broader sense, related to studies like Caicedo (2019) and CastelloCliment, Chaudhary and Mukhopadhyay (2018) which show how missionaries helped in creating human capital in Latin America and India through establishing missionary schools. The present study’s focus is on the clergy initiated democratic involvement which could be

treated as a complementary public good to formal education incentives and having a different effect in mitigating small-scale skirmishes. The rest of the paper is organized as follows. In the following section, we briefly describe the connection between Church or clergy induced democratic participation and rural protest in Latin America. Section 3 outlines the empirical strategy including the data used. Section 4 presents the results and discussions. The mechanism results are presented in section 5. Finally, section 6 concludes the paper. In Appendix Tables section, we provide detailed empirical results from main tables in the manuscript. The Data Appendix sheds light on the data and coding decisions for the new channel variable, church or clergy-induced democratic participation. 2. Church or clergy initiated democratic participation and rural protests in Latin America We focus on democratic participation through Church or clergy-initiated activities like years of active Base Christian Communities ( Comunidades Eclesiales de Base or CEB) given the fact that religion plays an important role in Latin America (Smith, 2017; Bruneau, 1980). For example, t he latest Pew Report of Religion in Latin America (2014) 5 , shows that 69% adults are Catholic and 19% are Protestant when 30,000 respondents were surveyed in 2013-2014. Interestingly, close to 50% of both Christian faiths support charity work for the poor and around one-fifth of the Catholic respondents reported that they would persuade the government to help the poor. Democratic participation through religious activities is an important dimension as there is a well-established literature in political science which show how religion in general and Catholicism in particular as well as politics are interrelated in Latin America (Sanders 1982). Sanders (1982) reviews three books on the subject and points to heterogeneities across countries on the issue of Catholicism and politics. In particular, Sanders (1982, pg. 256) writes, “ The church, to be sure, defines a special public (and political) role for itself. One dimension involves the denunciation of injustice and exploitation and the promotion of a better society through several strategies: statements analyzing the problems and sketching general directions for society, the formation of CEBs and other organizations where participants can develop a social orientation based on Christian presuppositions, encouragement of the laity to be active politically, and church activities such as defense of human rights and the poor under authoritarian and oppressive systems, especially where other institutions cannot function. ” We exploit the ‘broader special public role’ dimension with democratic participation through church-initiated activities which would have a bearing on pacifying protests. These activities could be described as ‘human rights monitoring, organizing labor protests, establishing cooperatives and credit unions, providing health care and education, fundraising, supporting solidarity campaigns, publishing and other reform activities’ (CIA Report 1986). Thus, years of such participatory actions at the grassroot level could be seen as an impetus to significant policy decisions such as land reforms. Bruneau (1980) mentions how involvement in such grassroot organizations like the CEB in Brazil and other Central American countries are driving the local development agenda including establishment of production and consumer 5 https://www.pewforum.org/2014/11/13/religion-in-latin-america/

cooperatives and hiring lawyers to challenge arbitrary political and administrative measures. The CIA Report (1986) documents active role plays by CEBs in (i) Nicaragua in the 1970s “ to promote social action and apply pressure to Somoza government ”, (ii) El Salvador in the 1980s to give support to democratic reform initiatives, and (iii) Guatemala in the 1970s in the wake of widespread human rights abuse by the successive oppressive military regimes. One empirically measurable aspect of local participation in religious activities is the advent and effective duration of CEBs which were represented by mainly the poor and non-elitist people under the aegis of sympathetic bishops within the Catholic Church. 6 The effectiveness of CEBs were determined by the local clergy or bishops pivotal in organizing such communities at the diocesan levels as happened in a number of Latin American countries like in Nicaragua and Honduras. The CEBs have certain other unique characteristics such as (a) these were formed at the local levels and lead by lay people or the masses (and not elites), (b) the CEBs were institutionalized after the conclave of Catholic Church in 1968 at Medellin, Colombia and the initial efforts were made in earlier conference of Catholic Bishops and under the directive of Catholic Church in between 1962 and 1965; thus making the advent of CEBs exogenous to the large scale rural protests, 7 (c) the effective diocesan clergy were primarily from outside the domicile countries and (d) the CEBs were free from direct influence of other institutional directives like forming coalition to defend the spread of communist influence in 1965. A number of recent studies highlight how religious participation and democratic activities are interlinked with church participation treated as a broader civil society engagement (Vasquez and Williams, 2005; Smith, 2017). Both in developed countries like the USA (Verba, Nie and Kim, 1978; see also the classic study by Tocqueville, 1838) and developing countries like Chile (Bauwens and Lemaitre, 2014), church influenced social and democratic participation helped the needy, especially the poor and shaped subsequent development in a number of countries like the Democratic Republic of Congo (Kyamusugulwa and Hilhorst, 2015) and Guatemala (Wydick, Karp and Hilliker, 2011). Based on the discussion above, we also include additional markers of clergy directed or church induced democratic participation. First, following Wang (2021), we code the years of radio address by prominent local clergies eliciting participation in democratic activities as in Honduras where one of the progressive bishops, Mr. Evelio Dominguez started vigorous rural engagement through regular ‘radio talks’ from 1961 onward (Shepherd 1993). Second, publication of statements or newspaper articles soliciting sympathizers for social activities like in the Dominican Republic in the 1970s are also coded as democratic participation years (US Country Studies for Dominican Republic, 1989). Finally, if local clergy themselves participated in some social activities like joining labor unions as happened in Costa Rica

6 Note that the land reforms in the dataset do not include forcible expropriation of Church owned lands and redistributing it to the rural poor as happened in a number of Latin American countries (Mexico, 1857; Ecuador 1907) primarily in the late 19 th century or early 20 th century (Albertus 2015, pgs. 53-54). Hence the establishment of CEBs could not be treated as a reaction to such land reforms. 7 There are instances that clergies directing CEBs were involved in rural protests in countries like Nicaragua, Guatemala, El Salvador, Brazil, Chile (CIA Report 1986) as well as Honduras (Shepherd 1993). Eventually some of these active clergies formed political parties and joined the political process like the Christian Democratic Party in Guatemala.

during the time of Archbishop Sanabria’s tenure 8 (Brett, 1993), we also include these activity years within the realm of democratic participation as exemplars. 3. Empirical strategy and data 3.1. Event study analysis We start with the event study analysis which takes the following form: LLLLLLLL iiii = αα+ ∑ ββ jj llllllll jj=2 llll ii × 1{llllllll tt = jj} + ∑ ββ mm llllll mm=0 llll ii × 1{llllll tt = mm} + δδ. ZZ+ φφ ii + σσ tt + εε iiii (1) where rural protests is the outcome or dependent variable taken from Albertus (2015). These protests occur at different time epochs and could be treated as incidents of protests 9 akin to the incidents of civil conflict (Hull and Imai, 2013; Fearon and Laitin, 2003). The rural protest incidents are denoted by ‘ Lsrp ’. ‘ i ’ stands for country and ‘ t ’ refers to years. It is interesting to note that these large-scale rural protests lead to further civil conflict over time in a number of countries (Albertus, 2015). Thus, these rural demonstrations could be interpreted as low-intensity conflict incidents. The above is a flexible event study setting as in Jacobson, LaLonde and Sullivan (1993), Stevensons and Wolfers (2006) and Bhalotra, Clarke, Gomes and Venkataramani (2019). The flexibility is in relation to the specification addressing different start time of land reform implementations in different countries. This is similar to the spirit of Goodman-Bacon (2018). The main independent variable is denoted by lr , which represents land reform implementation at country i . lr takes the value of 1 if a country ever implemented a particular kind of land reform, and using the event study design, we interact this land reform with a set of leads and lags based on the year the land reform was implemented. We use 10 periods for leads and 10 periods for lags keeping in mind that these major land reform implementations are not occurring very frequently, and, thus, deciphering the impact of a particular land reform implementation within a period of 10 years before and 10 years after will be a reasonable timeframe to analyse its impact on the outcome variable. 10 In the model, we are also using appropriate controls as outlined later. In addition, we also control for unobservable factors using country fixed effects ( φφ ii ) and year fixed effects ( σσ tt ). The coefficients of interest will be the estimates of ββ llllllll as they would capture the impact after land reform implementations. The lag variables could check and test for any pre-trends which are very important in the underlying identification assumption of no parallel pre-trends (Bhalotra et al., 2019). 11 Freyaldenhoven et al. (2019) point that the absence of such pretrends will be taken as a test of exogeneity of the policy variable, which is the land reform implementation in our context (described in detail in the next paragraph). Note that this test would be judged as partial as it can only identify parallel pre-trends but could not actually test 8 1940-1952. Please refer to country-specific data details in Data Appendix. 9 The dependent variable takes the value 1 for years of protests, and 0 with years of no protests as documented in Albertus (2015). 10 Note that individual country level studies by Albertus and Kaplan (2013) and Albertus (2020) also use similar time spans. 11 The first lead is omitted as the baseline category.

for counterfactuals (Bhalotra et al., 2019; Kahn-Lang and Lang, 2018). The a priori expectation is that the overall impact of land reform implementations within the post 10-year window would reduce the likelihood of protest incidents. We exploit various motives of land reform implementations from Bhattacharya et al. (2019) which are relevant for Latin American countries. These are: (i) land reform implementation as a package subsuming all different strands of reforms as illustrated in Bhattacharya et al. (2019), (ii) redistribution motive where lands were redistributed to landless along with full end-user rights on land, (iii) a combination of land ceiling, land expropriation and land redistribution motives of land reforms as often these motives are implemented together, (iv) pro-poor land reforms where reforms are specifically targeted towards the poor or landless or land-poor or squatters as mentioned in the reform initiatives, so this can be treated as a package explicitly designed for the poor and (v) tenure security improvement as the sole reform motive where the land tenure arrangements are improved without transferring physical lands and end-user rights to land, In addition, the following land reform motive is taken from Albertus (2015), termed as global major/minor land reform, which “ is defined as the expropriation and redistribution of at least 10 percent of cultivable land over a continuous period with at least one year in which more than 1 percent of cultivable land is expropriated. ” 12 Minor reforms are depicted as “ those where there was expropriation and redistribution as part of a land reform program but at a threshold short of major reform. ” A number of relevant control variables (denoted by the vector ZZ ) are used based on the extant literature (Blattman and Miguel, 2010; Esteban et al. 2012). These include time-varying controls like one year lag of log GDP per capita, log of population, democracy indicator 13 as well as an oil exports indicator dummy 14 which may have any impact on the large-scale rural protests have also been controlled for. The novel variable, democratic participation through Church or clergy-initiated activities (CIDP) is also controlled for in the empirical analysis given their possible influence on rural protests. Moreover, time-invariant variables like non-contiguous territory and ethno-linguistic fractionalization as in Fearon and Laitin (2003) have also been controlled for. The standard errors are robust and clustered at the country level following the suggestion of Bertrand, Duflo and Mullainathan (2004) . The same event study approach is also used as the first checkpoint for placebo results with an alternative indicator of civil strife incidence used in the literature (Hull and Imai, 2013)) as outcome variable. Similarly, possible mechanisms (detailed later) are also analyzed with the above event study setting as the first touch point in the empirical analysis. 3.2. Baseline regression analysis Then, we carry out the baseline analysis with large scale rural protests as the outcome or dependent variable using the following econometric specification: 12 See notes of Albertus (2015), Table 8.1, page 273. 13 Please refer to the Data sub-section below for detailed description of the variable used. 14 Please refer to the Data sub-section below for detailed description of the variable used

LLLLLLLL iiii = ∝ 1ii + ββ 1tt + γγ. tr + δδ 1 . LLLL iiii + θθ 11 . ZZ+ εε 1iiii (2) The above empirical specification could be termed in the spirit of a two-way generalized difference-in-difference analysis as in Borusyak et al. (2022). The two-way effects are country-specific fixed effects ( ∝ 1ii ) and time effects ( ββ 1tt ) . Given the long-time span of the main explanatory variable (detailed description below) and the outcome variable (19302008), we also control for country-specific time trends in the above specification ( γγ. tr) . In this specification, the main explanatory variable is the cumulative effect of land reform implementations, denoted by LR . Given that the land reform episodes are major milestones in countries long-run trajectory (1900-2010, Bhattacharya et al. 2019; Albertus, 2015), it would be important to analyse the long-term consequence of such reforms on major protests witnessed in Latin America. Note that land reforms are not uniformly implemented in a number of countries in Latin America, for instance, in Argentina (Bhattacharya et al. 2019). In addition, even if there are multiple land reforms enacted over the years, they are always partially implemented as in Costa Rica, Guatemala and Honduras. Then in a number of other countries like Belize, Bolivia and Colombia, some particular land reforms are fully implemented, and others are either partially implemented or not implemented at all. 15 Thus, in the realm of difference-in-difference analysis, reforms which are fully or partially implemented could be used as the ‘treated’ group and the ‘control’ group will be reforms which are not implemented at all. Under the reform category, we have analyzed various motives of land reform implementations as outlined before in the event study analysis. In addition, the following motives of reforms from Bhattacharya et al. (2019) are also incorporated. These include, (i) reforms mandating upper limits in landholding or land ceiling, (ii) land expropriation motive where excess land over the mandated ceiling have been taken over from landlords for later reallocation to land-poor or landless people, and (iii) a combination of land reform motives where end-user rights in land are given, viz., (a) consolidation type reforms where land is mainly re-parcelled into unitary blocks for improved use with end-user rights in land granted; (b) distribution of land from state-owned farms to citizens; and (c) privatization or sale of state-owned farmlands to individuals. Moreover, the global national land reform from Albertus (2015) is used as an additional motive of land reform. We expect that land reform implementations, taken cumulatively would have a negative bearing on large scale rural protests. In line with the event study analysis, we have also used the same set of controls in equation (2), denoted by the vector Z . The standard errors are now robust to heterogeneity. Given the nature of the outcome variable with the protests taking values of 1 and 0 interchangeably, we can treat the rural protests to be happening with some probabilistic nature. Thus, we validate the robustness of baseline ordinary least squares results using Poisson model with fixed effects as an alternative estimation strategy. 16

15 Please refer to the Supplementary Appendix of Bhattacharya et al. (2019), and especially Table A1 for information about major land reforms enactment bills and their implementation status. 16 Note that negative binomial regression is another plausible model for checking robustness, however, we cannot estimate the negative binomial model incorporating fixed effects.

Note that the event study analysis and the baseline regression model presented above do not address possible endogeneity issues which are discussed below. 3.3. Addressing endogeneity with instrumental variable estimation A number of plausible sources of endogeneity have to be accounted for in order to achieve identification. The main concern is that land reform implementations themselves could be a bone of contention or protests for stakeholders (Albertus and Mendalo, 2012; de Janvry et al. 2015), leading to possible reverse causality and biased estimates. The other sources of endogeneity related to plausible model misspecification and omitted variables are of lesser concern given that we have controlled for a number of fixed effects, trends as well as additional time-varying and time-invariant factors which may instigate civil strife based on the extant literature. We use an instrumental variable strategy to address endogeneity concerns following the extant literature (Miguel, Satyanath and Sergenti, 2004; Blattman and Miguel, 2010). The instrumental variable is the years of elite split as measured by Albertus (2015). Albertus (2015) demonstrates that years of split between landed elite and political elite in the political hierarchy in Latin American countries could be used as an important driver of land reform decisions in Latin America. The implementations could be viewed as political economy verdicts with many different stakeholders like democratic/autocratic/military governments, elite citizens, rural masses, as well as urban groups involved in the reform and implementation initiatives (Bhattacharya et al, 2019, Albertus, 2015). Thus, years of elite split could be treated as relevant political economy instruments in line with the approach of Besley and Case (2000). Besley and Case (2000) employ an instrumental variable for a political confounder, similar to the present setup. We re-estimate the baseline specification in equation 2 using the instrumental variable two stage least squares method and this becomes our main specification in the empirical analysis. Looking at the large scale rural protests from Albertus (2015), which could be broadly categorized into the forms of land invasions (Dominican Republic, El Salvador, Honduras, Nicaragua, Panama, Venezuela), strikes (Argentina, Bolivia), protests (Chile, Paraguay), demonstrations (Bolivia, Mexico), blockades (Ecuador), movement of landless rural workers and farmers (Brazil) as well as rural insurgency (Colombia, Cuba, Guatemala, Mexico, Peru, Uruguay), there is no evidence that the protests are linked with elite split. Similarly, focusing on the broad outcomes of such protests listed in Albertus (2015), there is no link that such demonstrations lead to split between political elites and landed elites in Latin America. Thus, years of elite split should be safely categorized as one pure exogenous variation affecting land reform decisions and orthogonal to the rural protests. Thus, exclusion restriction should be treated as passed comfortably when employing years of elite splits as instruments for land reform implementations. In addition, we have performed placebo checks using instrumental variable estimation with conflict incidence as the outcome variable in equation 2. The key idea is that cumulative land reform implementations would reduce large-scale rural protests, and not necessarily conflict incidences as such conflicts could be linked with a number of reasons apart from rural protests or land reforms. Hence, we expect the signs of cumulative land reforms to be either insignificant or positive in the placebo check results.

The extant literature on civil conflict identifies income proxied by the level of GDP per capita or growth of GDP per capita as the main source of endogeneity (Blattman and Miguel, 2010; Verwimp et al. 2019). To address such endogeneity concerns, researchers use instrumental variables estimation with rainfalls or other measures as instruments for income, especially in the context of sub-Saharan African countries (Miguel et al. 2004; Ciccone, 2011). In this paper’s context, such concerns are futile given that the outcome variable is rural protests which are not driven by income concerns. Even if there are some plausible issues that per capita income could still be endogenous to rural protests, in the empirical analysis, we use one year lagged value of GDP per capita in log terms as in Fearon and Laitin (2003) to address such concerns that income per capita may be correlated with major rural protests. Note that Albertus et al. (2018) also employ one year lagged independent variables. In addition, we cross-checked our results excluding per capita income as a control in the baseline IV specifications and find that the main results still survive this robustness check. 3.4. Mechanisms In order to decipher possible channels behind the main findings, we resort to two different pathways using the instrumental variable setting. The first one sheds light on the amounts of land redistributed as a result of implemented reforms. The idea is that with lands transferred to land poor or landless or needy people, there are more productive income earning opportunities by either tilling lands and engaging in agricultural and related activities (Arnot, Luckert and Boxall, 2011) or using lands as collaterals to establish/enhance farming operations (Feder, 1988; Place, 2009; Deininger and Feder, 2001). These increases the opportunity cost of engaging in protests which would reduce the likelihood of joining such skirmishes. We use the following specification similar to equation (2) where the outcome variable is replaced as land redistributed in percentage terms taken from Albertus (2015). Given that land redistribution was one of the important land reform motives undertaken in Latin American region countries, it makes sense to pinpoint if the amount of land redistributed could work as a channel in reducing protest intensity. LLLLLLLL rrrrrrrrrrrr iiii = ππ ii + μ tt + Ø. tr + ƞ. LLLL iiii + θθ 22 . ZZ+ εε 2iiii (3) The proportion of lands redistributed due to land reform implementations would work as a mechanism to mitigate rural protests. We expect the signs of cumulative land reform implementations ( ƞ ) to be positive from equation (3) above. The second pathway sheds lights on reducing land inequality which is the highest in Latin American region countries (Binswanger et al. 2009). Here we expect that cumulative land reform implementations would make land distribution more egalitarian and hence the land inequality would diminish. This in turn would reduce the rural protests as the extant literature find a positive association between land inequality and conflict in general (Russett 1964, Verwimp, 2005, Albertus et al. 2018) and particularly in Latin America (Jensen and Sorensen, 2011). We employ the following specification with land gini coefficient as the outcome variable and the cumulative impact of land reform implementations as the main explanatory variable along with the controls used in the main specification in equation 2.

LLLLLLLLLLLLLLLL iiii = ππ 1ii + μ 1tt + θθ 1 . tr + ƞ 1 . LLLL iiii + θθ 33 . ZZ+ εε 3iiii (4) We expect the signs of cumulative land reform implementations ( ƞ 1 ) would be negative as land inequality would decline after such reforms. The data sources details are provided in the following sub-section. 3.5. Data 3.5.1. Dependent variable The main dependent variable is the large-scale rural protests (denoted by Lsrp in equations 1 and 2) taken from Albertus (2015). Albertus (2015) notes that such protests are the major ones, and we take these major protests in the analysis. The protest years are denoted by 1 and the non-protest years are assigned zeros. In deciphering the mechanisms, two separate dependent variables are employed in equations 3 and 4. These are (i) the amount of land distributed in percentage terms from Albertus (2005) (denoted by Land redist. in equation 3) and (ii) the land Gini inequality measures from Frankema (2010) (denoted by Landgini in equation 4). The original data for land gini are reported for a few years. We interpolated the intermediate years of data using a linear interpolation method, similar in spirit of Albertus et al. (2018). In the placebo test, the dependent variable is an alternative indicator of civil strife incidence, which are taken from the Armed Conflict Database of the International Peace Research Institute, Oslo, Norway and the University of Uppsala, Sweden. Miguel et al. (2004), Brückner and Ciconne (2011) and Hull and Imai (2013) employ this incidence of civil conflict measure in their empirical analysis. Following Hull and Imai (2013), we use conflict incidence as an indicator variable taking the value of 1 if there is an ongoing civil conflict in country i at time t which results into at least 25 deaths per year; else, it takes the value 0. 3.5.2. Independent variable The independent variables are various motives of land reforms implemented from Bhattacharya et al. (2019) and two particular motives of land reforms from Albertus (2015). All land reforms which are partially implemented from Bhattacharya et al. (2019) have been treated as fully implemented in the empirical analysis. The detailed description about the land reform motives has been provided earlier. 3.5.3. Control variables The novel control variable, democratic participation through Church or clergy-initiated activities (CIDP) is constructed using a number of relevant resources as detailed in the Data Appendix. The years of CIDP or active CIDP are coded as 1, else it takes the value 0. The other control variables are selected based on the extant literature (Blattman and Miguel, 2010; Esteban et al. 2012). These are: (i) one year lag of log GDP per capita from Maddison (2009) database, (ii) log of population from Maddison (2009) database, (iii) democracy indicator which takes the value of 1 if the relevant polity2 score is equal to or above 5, and 0, otherwise based on Esteban et al. (2012), (iv) oil exports indicator dummy which takes the value of 1 if a country’s oil export revenue in that year is at least 1/3 of total export revenue based on Fearon and Laitin (2003), (v) a non-contiguous territory dummy based on Fearon (2003) which is given a value of 1 if any country with a territory of at least 10,000 people and

separated from the mainland containing the capital city either by land or by 100 kilometres of water, else 0 and (vi) the ethno-linguistic fractionalization measure from Fearon (2003). 4. Results and discussion Figures A to D lay out the results from event study analysis using equation 1. Tables 2 to 11 present the results from empirical analysis along with various robustness checks and placebo test findings. Table 1 contains the summary statistics. 4.1. Results from event study analysis Figures A (Figure 1 to Figure 6) report the findings with large-scale rural protests as the outcome variable. A quick look at Figure 1 shows that there are no pre-trends, and the post land reform implementations are reducing protests after two years of reforms, especially between years 3 to 7. If we consider the redistributive reform in Figure 2, however, there is no evidence of rural protest mitigation after such land reform implementation within the 10year time period. This is quite plausible as generally it takes a bit more time (or more than the stipulated 10-year window selected here) to absorb the impact of redistributive reforms. Now, focusing on combined land ceiling, expropriation and redistributive reforms together in Figure 3, again, there is no pre-trend before the land reform implementation window. Similar to Figure 1 above, the post land reforms years show ameliorating effect for rural protests for the lead periods of years 5 to 7. The most interesting finding is related to the global major/minor land reform by Albertus (2015) in Figure 4. Here we see clear mitigating impact throughout the post 10-year reform implementation period. However, there is some evidence of negative pre-trend in the prereform period which raises some concerns on treating the land reform implementations as purely exogenous treatments. Now focusing on pro-poor land reform as a package in Figure 5, there seems to be muted intervention support as land reform implementations are statistically insignificant for all leads except for years 5 to 7. Note that there is no evidence of pre-trends either in this particular motive of land reform as well. Finally, when we shed light on tenure security improvement reforms in Figure 6, again, there is not statistically significance evidence on mitigating rural protests before or after ten years of such reforms being implemented. Note that tenure security improvement does not entail physical transfer of land, and hence the pacifying impact of such initiatives seems to be muted. Overall, the event study primer check in Figures A show some evidence of mitigating impact of land reform implementations (in four out of six different motives of land reform implementations analyzed) by focusing on these implementations as exogeneous treatments, especially after 5 to 7 years of such reforms being implemented. Figures B (Figure 7 to Figure 12) present findings from placebo analysis with conflict incidence as the outcome variable. At the outset, the focus here would be either no impact or maybe positive impact on conflict incidence in the post 10-year window of land reform implementations. Looking at Figure 7 with all land reforms implemented, we find largely statistically insignificant impact in the post reform period with some evidence of positive effect in further leads 6 and 7. Similarly, for redistributive reforms in Figure 8, there is positive impact from periods 6 to 9 in the post 10-year window and the rest of the periods

remain statistically insignificant. The combined motive of land ceiling, expropriation and redistribution in Figure 9 also point to the same narrative as the redistributive land reforms. The global major/minor reforms from Albertus (2015) in Figure 10 show clear support for our placebo test as well with the post reform period’s impact on conflict incidence predominantly positive and statistically significant and particularly from the years 3 to 9 in the post 10-year window. In line with the above, the pro-poor land reforms also reveal positive and statistically significant impact in the post reform lead periods of 6 to 9 with no significant impact beforehand. Thus, the placebo checks are in line with the conjecture that land reforms implementations would only ameliorate large-scale rural protests, but not conflict incidents which could be triggered by various reasons apart from rural skirmishes. Unfortunately, all the above reforms are also plagued with statistically significant pre-trends. Thus, even if the post reform findings support the placebo conjecture, we need to be mindful that the reform implementations could not be used as exogenous treatments. Note that the evidence from tenure security improvement type land reforms in Figure 12 reveals no statistically significant impact both 10-year before and after such reform implementations, and hence lends credence to the falsification test conjecture that land reforms are going to ameliorate only rural protests and no other conflicts. Figures C (Figure 13 to Figure 18) report the first mechanism primer figures with land redistributed as the dependent variable. There is immediate support from Figure 13 that land reform implementations as a package remains statistically significant and positive in the post reform years without any pre-trends, thus, lending credence to the proposed channel in mitigating rural protests. The redistribution motive land reforms from Figure 14 also show positive support to the proposed mechanism as most of the post reform years reveal positive and statistically significant land redistributions. There are no pre-trends, and hence, redistributed reforms could be termed as exogenous treatments. Similarly, taking a look at the combined land ceiling, expropriation and redistributive motive reforms together in Figure 15, there is some muted support for positive land transfers at leads 2 and 3, but not for the whole 10-year post reform period. Note that there are no pre-trends in the pre-reform implementation time period as well. In line with the Figure 4 above, the major/minor reforms of Albertus (2015) in Figure 16 reveals clear and sustained improvement in land redistribution in the post 10-year reform period without any evidence of pre-trends. Thus, major/minor reforms of Albertus (2015) could be used as an exogenous treatment to decipher the plausible mechanism. However, looking at the pro-poor land reforms implementations exclusively, there is no support that such land reform implementations lead to transfers of land even within the 10year time period though there is clear evidence of no pre-trends in the pre reform implementation period. On the other hand, interestingly, tenure security improvement is now showing positive and statistically significant relationship with land redistribution within the 10-year post reform period without any pre-trends. Hence tenure security improvement could be used as a plausible mechanism. To summarize, focusing on Figures C, there is very good support that land reform implementations could be used as exogeneous treatments to tease out the potential mechanism as in all cases, there are no pre-trends. Moreover, in five out of six different

motives analyzed in the event study setting, there is positive support for land redistributions in the post reform period. Figures D (Figure 19 to Figure 24) present the event study primer results for second pathway where land gini coefficient is the outcome variable with the treatment being various land reforms implemented. A quick look at all the land reforms implemented as a package in Figure 19, however, does not support this conjectured channel. Similarly, there is no support from the perspective of redistributive land reform (Figure 20), the combination of land ceiling, expropriation and redistributive reforms taken together (Figure 21), the global major/minor reforms from Albertus (2015) (Figure 22), pro-poor land reforms (Figure 23) as well as the tenure security improvement focused reforms (Figure 24). The no statistical significance could be attributed to the fact that distribution of landholding patterns takes a long time for adjustment, and hence analyzing within the ten years’ time interval is a relatively short period for revealing any statistically significant impact. 4.2. Findings from regression models Table 2 shows the findings from least squares regression which is estimated in the spirit of a two-way generalized difference-in-difference model after controlling for country-specific fixed effects and time effects. The focal variable of interest is the cumulative effect of land reforms implemented with a number of heterogeneous motives as depicted in the rows of the tables. In Table 2, we report only the heterogeneous land reforms implemented along with the new control variable, the active years of democratic political participation (CIDP) including the years of active CEB. 17 Note that none of the land reforms implemented has statistically significant effect at the conventional level, though the signs are negative for all land reforms, except for the ‘Combined end use’ reform implementation. Only land reform implemented as a package (LR implemented) has the p-value of 0.13, pointing that the cumulative impact of land reform as a package (with all different motives subsumed) works as a pacifier for large scale rural protests only at 13% level of significance. The statistically insignificant findings could be attributed to possible endogeneity concerns due to omitted variables or reverse causality. Given that we have controlled for a gamut of fixed effects and other relevant variables, including a novel control, CIDP, the possibility of endogeneity arising out of omitted variables would be quite less. Nevertheless, it remains a possibility. In regard to reverse causality, i.e., whether large scale rural protests lead to land reform implemented rather than the other way around, there is no evidence of such incidents from the original data source (Albertus 2015). There could be some drives towards land reforms legislations being passed or enacted due to protests, however, the chance of implementing such reforms in a cumulative way would be a big ask. The new control variable, years of active clergy induced democratic participation (CIDP) coefficients reported in the table show one interesting fact: that years or active years of democratic participatory action on the ground, do indeed dampen large scale rural protests, albeit not in a statistically significant way except for the ‘Combined end use’ type land reforms (specification 10, Table 2). The CIDP estimate is very close to the 10% significance level when used as a control with land reforms implemented as a package (LR implemented), 17 The full set of results for all control variables are reported in the Appendix Tables.

redistributive land reforms (Redistributive), global national land reforms (LR national), which again incorporates expropriation and redistribution without looking at the extent of land being transacted) as well as in the land reforms implemented to improve tenure security (Tenure sec. improve). Next, in Table 3, we present the results from Poisson model with conditional fixed effects to check the robustness of the least squares findings. The results here are better than the least squares in two dimensions. First, unlike in Table 2, now three land reform implementations show statistically significant and negative effect. These are (i) global major/minor reforms, (ii) global national reform and (iii) the tenure security improvement type reforms with the most significant impact attributed to the global major/minor reforms at 99% level of significance. Interestingly, the combined end use land reform implementations are now associated with higher likelihood of protests, which is in deference to our conjecture. Second, the new control variable, CIDPs are consistently negative and statistically significant at 90% level in all specifications except for the global major/minor reform’s case. Thus, there is very good support from the Poisson model estimation that large-scale rural protests could be mitigated with particular types of land reforms where either land are physically transferred through expropriation and redistribution (the focus of global major/minor reforms and global national reforms) or through improvement in land tilling arrangement rights by enhancing tenure security. In addition, church induced democratic participation (CIDP) also works as important and direct pacifiers for rural protests. 4.3. Instrumental variable estimation results The instrumental variable (IV) estimation addresses endogeneity concerns where years of elite split is employed as an instrument for cumulative land reform implementations. Albertus (2015) reports that elite split is a strong predictor of land reforms in Latin America. To be specific, reforms where lands were physically transferred from landowners to landless, are particularly attributed to elite split between landed elites and political elites. The first stage of IV results is reported in Table 4 and the second stage of IV results are presented in Table 5. 4.3.1. First stage results Taking a look at Table 4, we find that elite split is a strong instrument for a number of heterogeneous land reform implementations except for the two, the land ceiling and the combined end use type reforms. The KP Wald F-statistic and the Cragg-Donald Wald Fstatistic both show that elite split is a relevant instrument given the F-statistics are much above the Stock-Yogo critical values. In terms of magnitude, the elite spilt coefficient has the highest value for the global major/minor reform (LR maj-min.) reform which is a land reform categorized in Albertus (2015). This makes sense as a large proportion of land reforms implemented in Latin America saw lands being expropriated and redistributed. Note that, even individually, the expropriation and redistributive land reforms from Bhattacharya et al (2019) in specifications 3 and 4 are also showing 99% statistical significance. In the same vein, if we look at the combined motive of land ceiling, expropriation and redistribution in specification 5, we find that elite split is also an important predictor of such reform combination. Ignoring the major/minor aspect of land reform of Albertus (2015) in specification 6, if we concentrate only on land redistribution of Albertus (2015) in specification 7 (LR national, which again incorporates expropriation and redistribution

without looking at the extent of land being transacted), there is also strong evidence that elite split is working as an appropriate instrument. Land reforms listed in specification 3 to specification 7 involve physical transfer of land to the beneficiaries. In specifications 1 and 8, land reform implemented as a package subsuming all motives (LR implemented) and land reform targeted to the poor (Propoor), respectively, include both physical transfer of land as well as improving land users rights (like improving tenure security arrangements or legalizing informal land tenure arrangements) without reassigning any physical land. The instrumental variable, elite split, is strongly correlated with such reforms as well. Similarly, the tenure security improvement motive could also be instrumented with elite split, though it did not pass one of the first stage F-tests, KP Wald rk F-stat. Overall, it is heartening to know that elite split works as a strong instrument for a number of different motives of land reforms, be it involving physical transfer of land or transfer of some users rights, as well as combination of both types of categories. One plausible reason regarding the two land reforms which did not pass the first stage diagnostics (land ceiling and combined end use land reforms) would be that these reforms were implemented sparsely in Latin America. Lipton (2009) and Bhattacharya et al (2019) mention that land ceiling motive land reforms were prevalent in South Asian countries, and not in the Latin American countries. The combined end use land reforms are the type of reforms where end user rights to lands were transferred to the beneficiaries. These are the most progressive types of land reform in the sense that the beneficiaries not only have physical possession of land, but also enjoy total control of landownership and they are free to use these lands in any way they want. Based on Bhattacharya et al. (2019), these types of land reforms are land consolidation, land distribution, land privatization and land restitution. Again, these types of reforms were hardly implemented in the data sample. The results from the new control variable, CIDP, are also presented in Table 4. The idea here is that the active democratic participatory organizations including CEBs would work as facilitators for land reform implementation activities. The CEBs undertook a number of different political undertakings apart from offering prayers such as ‘human rights monitoring, organizing labor protests, establishing cooperatives and credit unions, providing health care and education, fundraising, supporting solidarity campaigns, publishing and other reform activities’ (Bruneau, 1980; CIA Report 1986). Thus, years of CIDPs could be seen as an impetus to big ticket decisions like land reforms. In line with the above conjecture, we find that for global major/minimum reforms (LR maj-min.), global national reforms (LR national) and expropriation motive reforms, CIDP plays statistically significant and positive roles. The CIDP also shows positive signs for a number of other reforms like Redistributive and Propoor but remains statistically insignificant. Given the nature of reforms involving physical transfer of land (expropriation and redistribution), it appears that year of active CEBs played important roles in implementing such decisions. Bruneau (1980) mentions how CEBs in Brazil and other Central American countries are driving the local development agenda including establishment of production and consumer cooperatives and hiring lawyers to challenge arbitrary political and administrative measures. The CIA document (1986) highlights active role plays by CEBs in (1) Nicaragua in the 1970s “ to promote social action and apply pressure to Somoza government ”, (2) El Salvador in the 1980s to give support to democratic reform initiatives, and (3) Guatemala in the 1970s in the wake of widespread human rights abuse by the successive oppressive military regimes. Note that for El Salvador

the large scale land reforms were not implemented (Kay, 2001) unlike in other countries like Mexico, hence CIDPs could instigate such decisions. 4.3.2. Second stage results The second stage results from the IV regression are presented in Table 5. The outcome variable is the years of large-scale rural protests (denoted by Lsrp ). All ten different land reform implementation coefficients are reported. Note that first stage results are passed for all models expect for model 2 (land ceiling land reform motive) and model 10 (combined end use motive land reform). There is partial support for first stage in model 9 involving tenure security improvement type land reforms as the Cragg-Donald Wald F-statistic value is more than the threshold level but the KP Wald rk F-statistic value is less than the threshold level. Looking at the estimated coefficients, there is overwhelming support for cumulative land reform implementations to be used as protest mitigators as in all cases, except for the combined end use motive, the land reform implementations are showing negative and statistically significant effect on rural protests. The land reforms used as a package subsuming all different motives of reforms (LR implemented) in model 1 reveal that a standard deviation increase in the cumulative land reform implementation reduces demonstrations by 0.911, i.e., 0.81 standard deviations of the large-scale rural protests. Now looking at pro-poor land reforms (Propoor) from model 8, the magnitude comes to reduction of 0.86 standard deviations of the large-scale rural protests due to a standard deviation increase in the cumulative pro-poor land reform implementation. The magnitudes are relatively large. The findings here are in line with the earlier short-term conjecture from the event study analysis in Figures A. Thus, it is interesting to note that within a short time horizon in the event study setting and using a significantly large time window in the endogeneity corrected IV analysis, there is very good support that land reforms could be used to mitigate low-intensity conflicts like the large-scale rural protests. This resonates with the studies like Albertus and Kaplan (2003) for Colombia and Albertus (2020) for Peru. Now focusing on land reforms where lands were physically transferred through redistribution (Redistributive, model 4), it reduces large-scale rural protests by 0.67 of standard deviation. Similarly, one standard deviation increase in the cumulative expropriation type land reform implementation in model 3 diminishes protests by around 0.90 of standard deviation. Taking landceiling, expropriation and redistributive motives together in model 5, it leads to a reduction of protests by 0.88 of standard deviation. Hence, land reforms where lands are physically transferred lead to even higher mitigation of large-scale protests than the reforms subsuming different motives or pro-poor reforms. Taking a look at the global major/minor reforms in model 6, there is 0.19 of standard deviation decline in the large-scale rural protests. Similarly, the one standard deviation increase in the global national reform implementation leads to 0.24 of standard deviation mitigation of rural protests. Note that these two types of motives incorporate expropriation and redistribution type reforms together with a threshold level of land being transferred. In such instances also, we find support that land reform implementations mitigate rural protests effectively. The tenure security improvement reforms enhance tenants’ engagement in the land tilling arrangements with landowners without transferring physical lands or full end users’ rights in

lands as owners. Yet, in such instances also, the rural protest is dampened by the highest extent, 2.51 of standard deviation. This is an interesting finding as the event study results show no statistically significant impact within the 10-year before or after window from the event study setup. Hence, it takes a longer time to realize its dampening impact on the largescale rural protests. The clergy induced democratic participation (CIDP) including active years of CEB also dampens large-scale rural protests by 0.03 of standard deviation when used as a control in model 1 with all motives of land reforms implemented (LR implemented). The finding reinforces our conjecture that church or clergy induced democratic participation ameliorates protests directly when used as a control in the second stage of instrumental variable analysis. The rest of the CIDP coefficients from other models (model 2 to model 10), however, are not statistically significant though they remain always negative in terms of their signs. A quick look at the other controls in the appendix Table 3A reveals that one lag of real GDP per capita (GDPl1) also works as a dampener in reducing protests. This is in line with a large number of papers in the extant literature on conflict (Blattman and Miguel, 2010; Verwimp et al. 2009) which establish that income is one of the most important mitigation variables for civil strife. The other interesting finding is involving oil exporting dummy (Oil) which shows that more export revenue from oil is associated with a decline in rural protest incidences except for model 2 (landceiling motive) and model 10 (combined end use motive) which incidentally did not pass the first stage restrictions. Also note that democracies (Democracy) are associated with more rural protests, i.e., democratic regimes enhance rural demonstrations in Latin American countries. However, the magnitudes of such democratic regime effects are small. 4.4. Findings from the placebo test Tables 6 and 7 lay out the results from placebo test with conflict incidence as the alternative outcome variable. Table 6 provides the first stage results and Table 6 reports the second stage results. These falsification evidence will bolster the exogeneity property of the instruments in the sense that we expect that the instruments (elite split) used for land reform implementations would only pacify the rural protests in the second stage, but these would not mitigate conflict incidences in general and may even aggravate such conflict incidences at the second stage when instrumented for land reform implementations. The first stage results from Table 6 reinforces the veracity of elite splits as strong and relevant instruments for land reform implementations in all models except for land ceiling and combined end use motive reforms (note that these are not reported in Table 6). The elite splits are strong and relevant instruments in all different motives of land reforms with the first stage estimates are statistically significant at 99% and all the relevant first stage test statistics are passed comfortably. The findings are in line with first stage main results from Table 4 earlier. Interestingly, the novel control variable, CIDP also shows positive and statistically significant effect for expropriation and global major/minor reforms, similar to main findings from Table 4. Note that in contrast to Table 4 results, now in case of global national land reform, the CIDP is not statistically significant though the sign becomes positive.

The second stage results from Table 7 reveal that various land reform implementations are instigating more conflict incidents when instrumented with elite splits. The findings immediately support the falsification exercise that cumulative land reforms implementations would mitigate only large-scale rural protests, but not conflict incidents. The second stage results are in line with de Janvry et al. (2015) for Mexico and Albertus and Kaplan (2013) for Colombia. Reconciling the magnitudes from Table 7 with the main findings from Table 5, we find that for cumulative land reform implementation as a package (LR implemented) instigate conflict incidence by 0.66 of standard deviation. Note that from the main results table (Table 5), one standard deviation increase in LR implemented leads to rural protests reduction by 0.81 of standard deviation. Hence, cumulative land reforms implemented have a net reduction effect of 0.15 of standard deviation. Thus, the falsification or placebo conjecture is established in terms of the magnitudes as well. Similarly, considering pro-poor land reforms as a package (Propoor), there is 0.61 of standard deviation increase in the conflict incidence, however, from the main findings in Table 5, the cumulative pro-poor land reform implementations are associated with 0.86 of standard deviation mitigation in rural protests. Hence, in this instance also, there is overwhelming evidence of net reduction in rural protests which bolsters the placebo evidence. In line with the above, we find net reduction in rural protests (reported in parentheses) for all other land reform implementations involving physical transfers of land, viz., expropriation (0.37 of standard deviation), redistributive (0.25 of standard deviation) as well as for combination of land ceiling, expropriation and redistribution (0.26 of standard deviation). Interestingly, for two other reforms where lands were physically transferred as well, viz., the global major/minor reforms and the global national reforms, the net reduction in magnitudes is closer to zero, i.e., the effect of such reforms are reducing the large-scale rural protests and instigating the conflict incidences at the same extent. Looking at the tenure security improvement reforms, there is also net reduction in rural protests evidence of 1.04 of standard deviation due to such cumulative reforms. This is a very interesting finding as in such instances, there is enhancement of land tilling arrangements without transferring end-user rights in land use. Also note that the clergy induced democratic participation (CIDP) is never statistically significant and remains mostly positive in Table 7. This provides some support to our placebo analysis as well in the sense that this novel instrument is only mitigating rural protests in the second stage main analysis (Table 5, all coefficients negative) and does not affect the conflict incidence at all.

5.1. Mechanism I: Improvement in percentage of land redistributed Table 8 and Table 9 provide results from first stage analysis and second stage estimation using equation 3 with percentage of land redistributed as the dependent variable and cumulative land reforms implemented as the main explanatory variables. Given the main findings that cumulative land reforms do dampen large scale rural protests, it is natural to figure out a plausible channel or mechanism on how such mitigating impact could be transpired through.

Taking a look at the first stage results (Table 8), there is overwhelming support that elite splits are very effective, strong and relevant instruments for cumulative land reforms implementations, a consistent finding reported throughout the empirical analysis in the paper. Also, the clergy induced democratic participation (CIDP), the novel control variable, remain positive and statistically significant predictor of such reforms for two motives, expropriation and global major/minor types of initiatives. For all other motives barring LR implemented, the CIDP variable remains positive though not statistically significant. Table 9 estimation results provide unequivocal support to percentage of land redistributed due to cumulative land reforms implementations as a credible mechanism. In all eight different models involving a variety of reforms motives, we find that reforms lead to positive and statistically significant land transfers. We can interpret these transfers in terms of extensive margins, i.e., lands redistributed provided additional opportunities for land tillers in engaging productive income earning opportunities which subsequently mitigated widespread rural protests. In regard to magnitudes, one standard deviation increase in the cumulative land reform implemented as a package (LR implemented, model 1) leads to 0.09 of standard deviation increase in land transferred. Given that the standard deviation of land redistribution is quite high (3.5827, Table 1), the above magnitude could be interpreted as 0.32 standard deviation increase in land redistributed which is a reasonable amount of land being transferred as a result of such cumulative reforms. Similarly, a standard deviation increase in the cumulative pro-poor land reform implementations as a package (Propoor, model 7) lead to a 0.37 standard deviation increase in land redistribution. Note that both of these magnitudes of the land reforms explain around half of mitigating effects (0.81 and 0.86 of standard deviations) from the main analysis in Table 5. Thus, lands transferred as packages remain very effective as mechanisms in reducing large-scale rural protests. Now focusing on other motives involving physical transfers of lands, there is 0.41 of standard deviation increase in land transfers as a result of expropriation (model 2), 0.42 of standard deviation increase in land transfers due to redistribution (model 3), 0.37 of standard deviation improvement in land transfers due to combined land ceiling, expropriation and redistribution motive land reforms (model 4), 0.05 of standard deviation increase in land transfers due to global major/minor reforms (model 5) and 0.08 of standard deviation improvement in land transfers due to global national land reforms (model 6). Amongst these motives, it is not surprising that redistributive motive is associated with the highest magnitude of land transfers as these transfers were largely attributed with redistribution. Taken together, there is overwhelming support for land redistribution as a channel as all of the above land reforms are reducing large-scale rural protests in the main analysis highlighted before (Table 5). 18 The above findings in terms of land redistributions have traction from the extant literature (Binswanger et al. 2009; Kay, 2001). Kay (2001) highlights the heterogeneities in terms of land transfer in Latin America with countries like Mexico, Guatemala, Chile, Peru and

18 Note that the tenure security improvement land reform is also leading to land transfers. However, given the first stage coefficients of such reforms are not of very high magnitude, we can safely ignore the magnitude involving such transfers.

Guatemala have embarked on large-scale land redistributions whereas countries like El Salvador and Colombia did not transfer that much of land. Table 9 findings also highlight the role played by the novel control variable, the church or clergy induced democratic participation (CIDP) in regard to such land transfers. In all different motives of reforms, except for expropriation, there is positive and statistically significant effect of CIDP to land redistributions. The effect is the strongest when used with cumulative land reforms implemented as a package (LR implemented, model 1) with one standard deviation increase in CIDP causing around 0.10 of standard deviation increase in the natural log of land redistributions in percentages. This is followed by usage in conjunction with the combination of land ceiling, expropriation and redistributive reform (Ceiling, expro. & redist, model 4) with one standard deviation increase in CIDP leading to around 0.08 of standard deviation increase in natural log of land transfers in percentages. The CIDP control also remains influential when employed with redistributive (model 3) and propoor land reforms (model 7) with the magnitudes of 0.07 of standard deviation increase in natural log of land transfers in percentages and 0.06 of standard deviation improvement in natural log of land redistributions in percentages, respectively. In contrast, the CIDP variable, when used with global major/minor reforms (model 5) and global national land reforms (model 6), show a bit lesser impact in terms of land redistribution percentages (around 0.04 of standard deviation increase and around 0.06 of standard deviation, respectively), but the effect remains positive and significant. Note that church induced democratic participation involved multifaceted activities in various dimensions like setting up production and consumer cooperatives, hiring lawyers to challenge arbitrary political and administrative decisions, fighting against human rights abuses as well as lobbying for democratic reforms (Jordan, 2022). The CIDP results here show one such manifestation through transfer of land which is quite remarkable. 5.2. Mechanism II: Reduction in landgini coefficient The second mechanism sheds light on reduction in land distribution through cumulative implementations of such major land reforms. The intuition is that with significant reform implementations which entails transfers of land, the land distribution would improve over time, thus reducing the land inequality manifested by the land gini coefficients. Albertus, Brambor and Ceneviva (2018) present latest evidence on land inequality in a number of Latin American countries and find that the relationship between land inequality and rural unrest in Brazil is quite unique in the sense that one-off land invasions are positively linked with land inequality; however, sustained land invasion threats could be mitigated by landowners, thus showing a negative relationship between rural skirmishes and land inequality. Our channel idea is rooted with the later evidence from Albertus et al (2018) as we expect that major land reforms would reduce landholding inequality and this in turn would reduce the likelihood of large-scale rural protests. This is in spirit with the famous ‘greed vs. grievance’ hypothesis put forward by Collier and Hoeffler (2004). The first stage results are presented in Table 10 and in Table 11, we lay out the second stage results. The first stage results from Table 10 again supports the elite splits employed as instruments for various land reform implementations.
