The effect of the split is the highest for global major/minor reform followed by the global national land reform. The magnitude for land

reform as a package, pro-poor land reform as a package and expropriation reforms remains very similar when instrumented with elite split. The lowest magnitude is attributed to tenure security improvement type reforms. The church or clergy induced democratic participation (CIDP) remains positive and statistically significant for global major/minor reforms and expropriation type reforms. This finding is in line with first stage mechanism results reported in Table 8. Thus, CIDP matters in pushing through such land reforms implementations. Note that the coefficients for CIDPs remain positive for all other reform implementations though these are not statistically significant. The second stage results from Table 11 reveal immediate support to our conjectured mechanism. In line with a priori expectation from equation 4, we find that the coefficients associated with various land reform implementations are always negative and statistically significant. Hence, these cumulative land reforms implemented reduced land inequality in a statistically significant way. Reconciling the first and second mechanism results, we can interpret the findings from Table 11 as ‘intensive margin’ results akin to the trade literature in the sense that not only lands were transferred (as per the first mechanism, could be termed as extensive margin), but such transfers actually made the land distributions more egalitarian. Focusing on the magnitudes, a one standard deviation increase in the cumulative land reforms implemented as a package (LR implemented, model 1) is reducing the land gini by 0.005 of standard deviation. This magnitude remains exactly the same when we look at the pro-poor land reforms as a package in model 7. Though the estimates seem to be small, but from the context of Latin America where the land gini coefficient is quite high (the average for our data sample is 0.76), even these small changes would have bigger impacts in the long run. The findings here support earlier conjecture of Wood (2003) that land reforms would work as a deterrent to rural protests by reducing land inequality. Note that Albertus and Kaplan (2013) and Albertus (2020) investigate the heterogeneities between small-scale and largescale land reforms and civil conflict in Colombia and Peru, respectively and highlight that only large-scale land reforms which were implemented had mitigating effect on civil strife. Given that these major reforms implementations were marred with some political interests, the subsequent reductions in land gini coefficients could not be that much as our results depict. Taking a look at the reforms where lands are transferred physically, we find that one standard deviation increase in redistributive reforms in model 3 leads to around 0.004 of standard deviation reduction in land gini coefficient. Similarly, expropriation type reforms cumulative implementation (model 2) leads to around 0.004 of standard deviation reduction in the land gini coefficient. The combination of land ceiling, expropriation and redistribution reforms in model 4 also reduces land gini coefficient by 0.005 of standard deviation. The two other reforms entailing transfers of land, viz., global major/minor reform (model 5) and global national reform (model 6), also diminishes the land gini by the same extent, 0.002 of standard deviation. There is a bit of heterogeneity in terms of reduction in land gini coefficient, but this is expected given the diversified nature of the reforms pursued. Interestingly, tenure security improvement motive reforms are also reducing land gini coefficient by 0.01 of standard deviation which shows that enhancing land tilling arrangements bode well for

landholding distributions. The findings resonate with conjectures from Huntington (1968) and Kalyvas (2006). Note that clergy or church induced democratic participation (CIDP), the novel control variable, is not statistically significant in any of the eight models used. In addition, their magnitudes are closer to 0. This is quite plausible as CIDPs open up some democratic windows of opportunities for supporting policies such as land reforms implementations as was manifested in the first stage results (Table 10); however, such involvement could not be directly transpired to changing land distributions and hence affecting the land gini coefficients subsequently.

The study analyses land reforms as potential pacifiers of large-scale rural protests in Latin America. The rural protests were quite prevalent and could be termed as low-intensity civil strife given that human casualties were largely absent. We investigate if major land reforms which were implemented and their cumulative impact in mitigating such skirmishes in the region over a long period of time. Exploiting a gamut of heterogenous land reforms from Bhattacharya et al. (2009) and Albertus (2015) and controlling for a number of potential confounders including time-varying controls and a host of fixed effects as well as time trends, we find robust evidence that cumulative land reforms implemented have mitigating effect on large-scale rural protests. The findings are in line with Albertus (2020) and Albertus and Kaplan (2013). The empirical strategy sheds lights on both short-run effects as well as long-run implications. The short-run effects are revealed with a flexible event study analysis with land reforms implemented as treated and no reforms implemented as control groups within a 10-year before and after window. Even within this short time span, we find some support that land reforms remain effective as a protest mitigation mechanism. The full scale long-run analysis using two-stage least squares employ years of elite spilt from Albertus (2015) as exogenous instruments for land reforms implemented. Amongst the land reforms, the strongest impact is attributed to the redistributive and expropriating type reforms individually as well as the combination of such reforms implemented jointly. In addition, we find that tenure security improvement type reforms which do not entail physical transfer of land and focus on enhancing tenure arrangements between land tillers and landowners also has a detrimental impact on large-scale rural protests. We propose a novel control variable, the church or clergy induced democratic participation (CIDP) including the years or active years of base Christian communities which provides a positive impetus to land reform implementations in our first stage instrumental variable analysis. Consequently, the coefficients of CIDPs become negative in the second stage instrumental variable regressions, lending credence to the idea that active years of democratic participation have a direct and soothing impact on large-scale rural protests. In the paper’s context, the CIDP heralds in a new democratic window of opportunity. This evidence goes well with the fact that Church participation plays an important role in shaping socioeconomic and political decision making in Latin America.

In regard to mechanisms, we pinpoint two plausible pathways involving proportion of land redistributed from Albertus (2015) and reduction in land inequality using data from Frankema (2010). First, there is strong evidence that land reforms implemented redistributed lands to the needy, thus creating more productive income earning opportunities (akin to the extensive margin type argument from international trade), which in turn reduced rural protests. Similarly, the land inequality gini coefficients show statistically significant reductions in land holding distributions, akin to an improvement in intensive margin of land holding patterns. In line with the main results, redistributive and expropriation type reforms as well as their combinations are associated with both higher proportions of land being redistributed as well as more reductions in land inequalities. Thus, these motives are attributed to higher protest mitigations in the main analysis. This paper’s investigation complements the extant literature on land reform and civil strife in Latin America by analysing the cumulative impact of major land reform implementations as a plausible tool to mitigate low intensity conflict manifested by large-scale rural protests. Given the huge debate surrounding the efficacy of land reforms to ameliorate rural skirmishes in this very important region of the world, the study sheds lights on possible pathways through which small scale protests could be mitigated.
